text: While several Chinese cities, including Xiamen, Dalian, and Ningbo, have successfully hosted peaceful anti-PX (para-xylene) protests in recent years, the 2014 demonstrations in Maoming emerged as a violent and noteworthy exception. Drawing on participant observation and in-depth interviews, this article explores why the Maoming movement took such a different trajectory. We find that despite intensive official propaganda, Maoming residents possessed a sophisticated understanding of environmental risks, rooted in their daily experiences with industrial pollution and their awareness of successful activism elsewhere. Unlike earlier middle-class movements, however, the Maoming protest was largely leaderless and spontaneous, a factor that contributed to its volatile nature and the subsequent state repression. Finally, we demonstrate how the movement managed to achieve large-scale mobilization—even without the backing of the middle class or mainstream media—by leveraging encrypted social media channels and a strategic use of the local urban landscape.
